title: Java Packaging Artifacts
description: Practice JAR creation, jlink images, unnamed modules, and automatic modules for Java 25 packaging objectives.
learningGoals: Create modular and non-modular JAR artifacts with the jar tool.
Use jlink with a module path to build a runtime image for named modules.
Distinguish unnamed class-path code from automatic modules on the module path.

completionChecks.prompt: Which command creates app.jar from compiled classes under out?
completionChecks.explanation: The jar tool creates JAR artifacts. jlink builds runtime images, java launches code, and javac compiles source rather than packaging classes.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens when util-1.0.jar has no module-info.class but is placed on the module path?
choices: It is ignored because only explicit modules can be on the module path
It becomes an automatic module with a derived module name
It joins the unnamed module exactly as it would on the class path
It forces every module to export all packages
completionChecks.explanation: A non-modular JAR on the module path is treated as an automatic module. A non-modular JAR on the class path belongs to the unnamed module instead.

completionChecks.prompt: Why does jlink need --add-modules with the application module?
choices: jlink links a runtime image starting from named root modules
jlink compiles .java files before packaging them
jlink converts every class-path JAR into source code
jlink runs completion checks from the lesson bank
completionChecks.explanation: jlink resolves named modules from the module path and links the requested roots plus their dependencies into a custom runtime image.
